verses.text: And David said, Is there yet any that is left of the house of Saul, that I may shew him kindness for Jonathan's sake? Guidance: The question is unprompted. David, now firmly established on the throne, with kingdom enemies subdued and cabinet in place, asks a question no one would have expected him to ask. 'Is there yet any that is left of the house of Saul, that I may shew him kindness for Jonathan's sake?' The first half of the question is dangerous in ancient Near-Eastern politics — surviving members of a previous regime were normally hunted down by the successor king to eliminate dynastic threats. The second half is the explanation: 'for Jonathan's sake.' Decades after the covenant in 1 Sam 20:14-15 — Jonathan asking David, 'Thou shalt not only while yet I live shew me the kindness of the LORD, that I die not: but also thou shalt not cut off thy kindness from my house for ever' — David remembers. He has not forgotten the friend who told him in the woods of Ziph 'thou shalt be king over Israel, and I shall be next unto thee' (1 Sam 23:17). The covenant David swore is binding on him now. Kindness as a kingdom-grade act: a king actively seeking out a descendant of his predecessor's house in order to bless him. The chapter is one of the most moving in the Old Testament because the action runs against every political instinct of the period. David is going to do this not because it serves the throne — politically it slightly threatens the throne — but because covenant-loyalty (chesed) survives the death of the covenant partner. 'For Jonathan's sake' — the phrase will recur three more times in the chapter. The chapter is teaching us what it looks like to keep a promise to someone who is no longer there to enforce it.

And there was of the house of Saul a servant whose name was Ziba. And when they had called him unto David, the king said unto him, Art thou Ziba? And he said, Thy servant is he. And the king said, Is there not yet any of the house of Saul, that I may shew the kindness of God unto him? And Ziba said unto the king, Jonathan hath yet a son, which is lame on his feet. And the king said unto him, Where is he? And Ziba said unto the king, Behold, he is in the house of Machir, the son of Ammiel, in Lo-debar. Guidance: A former household steward of Saul is located — Ziba, probably the senior estate manager of Saul's hereditary lands at Gibeah. He has survived the dynasty's collapse and probably still farms the old Saulide property. David asks again, now reframing the question: 'Is there not yet any of the house of Saul, that I may shew the kindness of God unto him?' 'The kindness of God' (chesed elohim) — not just human chesed, but the kind of covenant-loyalty God Himself shows. David identifies the source of the impulse he is acting on. Ziba answers: 'Jonathan hath yet a son, which is lame on his feet.' The reader's mind flashes back to 4:4 — the boy who was dropped at age five during the panic of the Gilboa news. Now grown. Still lame. Living in Lo-debar in the house of Machir son of Ammiel — across the Jordan, in trans-Jordan, in the same general region as Mahanaim where Ish-bosheth had been crowned. Lo-debar means literally 'no thing' — a place of nothingness, the small town where the surviving heir of the previous dynasty has been hiding out, expecting no future. Machir will reappear in chapter 17 as one of David's supporters during the Absalom rebellion; the kindness shown to Mephibosheth is going to be reciprocated when David needs help. The detail of 'lame on his feet' is the chapter's first reminder of Mephibosheth's status: politically invisible because physically disabled. The reader is meant to feel both pity for what life has done to this prince and the irony that the very disability that should have made him a non-threat to David is going to give him the open door to David's table.

Then king David sent, and fetched him out of the house of Machir, the son of Ammiel, from Lo-debar. Now when Mephibosheth, the son of Jonathan, the son of Saul, was come unto David, he fell on his face, and did reverence. And David said, Mephibosheth. And he answered, Behold thy servant! Guidance: David sends royal messengers across the Jordan to fetch Mephibosheth. The escort would have looked terrifying — official king's men arriving at the door of a known Saulide refuge. Mephibosheth, who has spent his adult life expecting that David would eventually find him and execute him for being a potential rival, is being summoned. He arrives at the palace. 'He fell on his face, and did reverence.' He prostrates himself — the standard posture for someone whose life depends on the king's mercy. He cannot run because of his disability; he can only bow. The narrator's identification of him is striking: 'Mephibosheth, the son of Jonathan, the son of Saul.' Three names, three generations — the lineage that produced this disabled survivor. David's first word is just his name: 'Mephibosheth.' One word, no titles, no preamble. Mephibosheth answers with the standard servant-formula: 'Behold thy servant!' (hinneh avdekha — the same phrase Samuel said as a boy to the LORD in 1 Sam 3). The moment is built on a thousand-fold misunderstanding — Mephibosheth thinks the moment of his death has come, and David is about to grant the moment of his restoration.

Then king David sent, and fetched him out of the house of Machir, the son of Ammiel, from Lo-debar. Now when Mephibosheth, the son of Jonathan, the son of Saul, was come unto David, he fell on his face, and did reverence. And David said, Mephibosheth. And he answered, Behold thy servant! Guidance: David sends royal messengers across the Jordan to fetch Mephibosheth. The escort would have looked terrifying — official king's men arriving at the door of a known Saulide refuge. Mephibosheth, who has spent his adult life expecting that David would eventually find him and execute him for being a potential rival, is being summoned. He arrives at the palace. 'He fell on his face, and did reverence.' He prostrates himself — the standard posture for someone whose life depends on the king's mercy. He cannot run because of his disability; he can only bow. The narrator's identification of him is striking: 'Mephibosheth, the son of Jonathan, the son of Saul.' Three names, three generations — the lineage that produced this disabled survivor. David's first word is just his name: 'Mephibosheth.' One word, no titles, no preamble. Mephibosheth answers with the standard servant-formula: 'Behold thy servant!' (hinneh avdekha — the same phrase Samuel said as a boy to the LORD in 1 Sam 3). The moment is built on a thousand-fold misunderstanding — Mephibosheth thinks the moment of his death has come, and David is about to grant the moment of his restoration.

And David said unto him, Fear not: for I will surely shew thee kindness for Jonathan thy father's sake, and will restore thee all the land of Saul thy father; and thou shalt eat bread at my table continually. And he bowed himself, and said, What is thy servant, that thou shouldest look upon such a dead dog as I am? Guidance: 'Fear not.' Two of the most pastoral words in 2 Samuel. David begins with reassurance because he knows what Mephibosheth has been expecting. Then the three-part covenant kindness. (1) 'I will surely shew thee kindness for Jonathan thy father's sake.' The motive is named — Jonathan, his father. Mephibosheth gets the inheritance of the covenant his own father had made with David thirty years earlier in the woods of Ziph. (2) 'And will restore thee all the land of Saul thy father.' The hereditary Saulide property — the family estates at Gibeah — are returned to the surviving heir. Mephibosheth becomes the legal landowner of his grandfather's property, even though his grandfather had been the king the LORD rejected and David replaced. The grant is enormous. (3) 'And thou shalt eat bread at my table continually.' Daily meals at the king's own table. This is the most extraordinary gift in the chapter — Mephibosheth gets the status of a son of the king, not a tributary or pensioner. He will sit at David's table the same way Solomon and Absalom sit at it. Mephibosheth's response: 'What is thy servant, that thou shouldest look upon such a dead dog as I am?' He uses the same image David himself had used at his own lowest moments (1 Sam 24:14, when David said to Saul 'after a dead dog'). The chapter is doing something beautiful with this image: Mephibosheth, who has actually been treated as a dead dog by political fate, is being lifted up by a king who knows what it feels like to be a hunted dog and refuses to do to others what was done to him. The wilderness years have produced a king who remembers what humiliation feels like and chooses chesed instead.

And David said unto him, Fear not: for I will surely shew thee kindness for Jonathan thy father's sake, and will restore thee all the land of Saul thy father; and thou shalt eat bread at my table continually. Then the king called to Ziba, Saul's servant, and said unto him, I have given unto thy master's son all that pertained to Saul and to all his house. Thou therefore, and thy sons, and thy servants, shall till the land for him, and thou shalt bring in the fruits, that thy master's son may have food to eat: but Mephibosheth thy master's son shall eat bread alway at my table. Now Ziba had fifteen sons and twenty servants. Guidance: David turns to Ziba and arranges the practical infrastructure. Ziba, who had been managing Saul's old estates for who-knows-whose benefit, is now formally assigned to manage them for Mephibosheth's benefit. 'Thou therefore, and thy sons, and thy servants, shall till the land for him, and thou shalt bring in the fruits.' Ziba's labor goes to Mephibosheth's account. The detail 'Ziba had fifteen sons and twenty servants' is included for two reasons: it tells us how substantial the agricultural enterprise will be (35 workers, plus Ziba), and it sets up chapter 16's complication, where Ziba will appear with great resources during David's flight from Absalom. The chapter's last clause repeats the key honor: 'Mephibosheth thy master's son shall eat bread alway at my table.' The dual provision — agricultural income from his own estate, plus daily royal table meals — gives Mephibosheth both economic independence and royal status. He is materially provided for and socially placed. The honor is total. The chapter is showing us that genuine kindness goes beyond the gesture; it includes the staffing and logistics that make the gesture sustainable. The chesed shown is a chesed that thinks about Mephibosheth's whole life going forward.

Then the king called to Ziba, Saul's servant, and said unto him, I have given unto thy master's son all that pertained to Saul and to all his house. Thou therefore, and thy sons, and thy servants, shall till the land for him, and thou shalt bring in the fruits, that thy master's son may have food to eat: but Mephibosheth thy master's son shall eat bread alway at my table. Then said Ziba unto the king, According to all that my lord the king hath commanded his servant, so shall thy servant do. As for Mephibosheth, said the king, he shall eat at my table, as one of the king's sons. Mephibosheth had a young son, whose name was Micha. And all that dwelt in the house of Ziba were servants unto Mephibosheth. Guidance: Ziba accepts the assignment. David repeats the most consequential part of the arrangement for the third time: 'Mephibosheth… shall eat at my table, as one of the king's sons.' The phrase 'as one of the king's sons' (ke-achad mibbene ha-melekh) is the formal designation. Mephibosheth is socially adopted into the royal household. He is not a guest at the table; he is a member of the family by the king's word. Then a small biographical note: 'Mephibosheth had a young son, whose name was Micha.' The Saulide line, which we thought had functionally ended at Gilboa, has a living descendant. Micha will be the ancestor of a substantial extended family — 1 Chr 8:34-40 lists his descendants through many generations. The line that David has just preserved through Mephibosheth will produce a real future. The chapter is showing us that David's chesed is not just to the surviving prince; it is to the dynasty's biological continuation. David could have eliminated the Saulide line; he has instead given it a future.

Then said Ziba unto the king, According to all that my lord the king hath commanded his servant, so shall thy servant do. As for Mephibosheth, said the king, he shall eat at my table, as one of the king's sons. Mephibosheth had a young son, whose name was Micha. And all that dwelt in the house of Ziba were servants unto Mephibosheth. Guidance: Ziba accepts the assignment. David repeats the most consequential part of the arrangement for the third time: 'Mephibosheth… shall eat at my table, as one of the king's sons.' The phrase 'as one of the king's sons' (ke-achad mibbene ha-melekh) is the formal designation. Mephibosheth is socially adopted into the royal household. He is not a guest at the table; he is a member of the family by the king's word. Then a small biographical note: 'Mephibosheth had a young son, whose name was Micha.' The Saulide line, which we thought had functionally ended at Gilboa, has a living descendant. Micha will be the ancestor of a substantial extended family — 1 Chr 8:34-40 lists his descendants through many generations. The line that David has just preserved through Mephibosheth will produce a real future. The chapter is showing us that David's chesed is not just to the surviving prince; it is to the dynasty's biological continuation. David could have eliminated the Saulide line; he has instead given it a future.

So Mephibosheth dwelt in Jerusalem: for he did eat continually at the king's table; and was lame on both his feet. Guidance: The chapter's final sentence is one of the most touching in the Old Testament. Two contrasts in one verse: 'Mephibosheth dwelt in Jerusalem' (he is in the capital, in the king's house, at the king's table) — and 'was lame on both his feet' (the disability that defined his life since age five remains). The chesed has not healed him. David has not magically restored his legs. The lameness persists. But what has changed is everything else around the lameness. The lame prince who could not have been a king, who hid in Lo-debar expecting death, now lives in Jerusalem and eats at the king's table every day for the rest of his life. The chapter is teaching us what grace actually does. Grace does not always heal the disability. Grace seats the disabled person at the table. The chapter is also a prefigure of the gospel: David, the LORD's anointed, seeks out the broken descendant of his enemy's house and seats him at his table for life because of a covenant promise made to a friend long dead. Christians have always read this chapter as a portrait of Christ's seeking out of broken sinners and welcoming them to the table because of a covenant promise. The chapter ends on this picture: a lame man at a royal table, fed daily by the king he had feared, made son by the king he had never expected to meet. Tomorrow, chapter 10, will return us to the world of foreign wars and political intrigue. Chapter 11 will begin the long fall of David's reign with the Bathsheba incident. But chapter 9 stands as one of the high points of the Davidic story — the moment when the king who would soon stumble in his most famous sin had also performed one of the most magnificent acts of covenant kindness in all of scripture. The chapter is the model of what David could be at his best.
